fact: September 1 is exactly 244 days into the year, which means there are exactly 121 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, September 1 marks the beginning of meteorological autumn, when temperatures start their big drop toward winter.
Monarch butterflies are typically in full migration mode around this time in North America, traveling up to 3,000 miles to escape the coming cold!
The sun rises about 2 minutes later each day around this time in most places, making mornings feel darker and sleepier.
September 1 falls in the middle of Virgo season for those who follow zodiac signs.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, September 1 is the first day of spring, when flowers start popping up everywhere!
Squirrels go absolutely nuts around this time in northern climates, frantically burying acorns and nuts to prepare for winter - they can bury up to 10,000 nuts!
The Northern Lights often become more visible starting around this date because the nights are getting longer and darker.
Apple trees are typically heavy with ripe fruit right now in temperate regions, and the smell of fallen apples starts filling orchards with that sweet, fermenting scent.
September 1 is roughly 3 weeks before the autumn equinox, when day and night will be exactly the same length everywhere on Earth.
Spiders seem to appear everywhere around this time because they're actually at their biggest size of the year after a summer of growing!
Early September often brings perfect conditions for those crisp, clear days with bright blue skies in many temperate regions.
